title: Organic Chemistry — Reactions & Mechanisms
description: Core organic chemistry reactions, mechanisms, stereochemistry, and functional group transformations for pre-med, MCAT, and undergraduate courses.
subject: chemistry

cards.front: SN2 reaction: mechanism, stereochemistry, and best conditions
cards.back: Bimolecular nucleophilic substitution. Backside attack → Walden inversion (inversion of configuration). Favors: primary substrates, strong nucleophile, polar aprotic solvents (DMSO, acetone, DMF). Rate = k[substrate][nucleophile]. Hindered substrates → E2 instead

cards.front: SN1 reaction: mechanism, stereochemistry, and best conditions
cards.back: Unimolecular — 2-step: carbocation intermediate forms, then nucleophile attacks. Racemization (both faces attacked). Favors: tertiary > secondary substrate, weak nucleophile, polar protic solvents (water, alcohols). Rate = k[substrate] (first-order kinetics). Carbocation stability: 3° > 2° > 1° > methyl

cards.front: E2 elimination: Zaitsev vs Hofmann products
cards.back: E2: concerted — anti-periplanar geometry required (H and LG on opposite sides of C-C bond). Zaitsev product: more substituted alkene (thermodynamic, preferred with small base). Hofmann product: less substituted alkene (bulky base: t-BuOK, or with quaternary ammonium LG)

cards.front: E1 elimination: energy profile and conditions
cards.back: Unimolecular — 2-step: carbocation forms first (rate-limiting), then base abstracts β-H. Favors: tertiary substrates, weak base, polar protic solvents. Carbocation rearrangements (hydride/methyl shifts) possible before elimination. Same conditions as SN1 but favored at high temperature

Markovnikov (HBr addition to alkene): H adds to less-substituted carbon, Br to more-substituted (more stable 2°/3° carbocation). Anti-Markovnikov: HBr + ROOR (peroxide, free-radical mechanism) — Br adds to less substituted C. Hydroboration-oxidation: anti-Markovnikov, syn addition

1. Base deprotonates α-carbon of ketone/aldehyde → enolate forms. 2. Enolate attacks carbonyl of second molecule → β-hydroxy carbonyl (aldol product). 3. Heating → dehydration → α,β-unsaturated carbonyl (condensation product). Used for C-C bond formation. Intramolecular: forms rings

Two esters (or ester + ketone = Claisen–Schmidt) + base → β-keto ester. Mechanism: enolate attacks ester carbonyl → tetrahedral intermediate → expel alkoxide → β-keto ester. Intramolecular: Dieckmann condensation (forms cyclic β-keto esters)

RMgX (alkyl magnesium halide) is a strong nucleophile and base. Reacts with: aldehydes/ketones → alcohols (adds C chain), CO2 → carboxylic acid, esters → tertiary alcohol (2 equivalents add). Must be anhydrous (water destroys Grignard). Prepared from R-X + Mg in ether/THF

Step 1: electrophile activated (e.g., Cl2 + FeCl3 → Cl+ equivalent). Step 2: Electrophile attacks ring → arenium ion (Wheland intermediate, carbocation). Step 3: Base removes H+ → rearomatization. EAS reactions: halogenation, nitration (H2SO4/HNO3), sulfonation, Friedel-Crafts alkylation/acylation

cards.front: Ortho/para vs meta directors in EAS: rule
cards.back: Activators (EDG — electron-donating groups): ortho/para directors. Examples: OH, OR, NR2, alkyl. Deactivators (EWG — electron-withdrawing): meta directors (except halogens: deactivating but ortho/para directors). Nitro, carbonyl, sulfonic acid, CN: meta directors

R-COOH + R'OH ⇌ R-COOR' + H2O (acid-catalyzed, reversible). Mechanism: acid protonates carbonyl → nucleophilic addition of alcohol → tetrahedral intermediate → eliminate water → ester. Drive equilibrium: excess alcohol, remove water (Dean-Stark trap). Reverse (saponification): NaOH + ester → carboxylate + alcohol (irreversible)

Reactivity toward Nu: Acid chloride (most reactive) > Acid anhydride > Ester > Amide (least reactive). Leaving group ability correlates with stability: Cl- > RCOO- > RO- > H2N-. Acyl chlorides easily converted to other derivatives. Amides are stable — require harsh conditions (strong acid/base) for hydrolysis

[4+2] cycloaddition. Diene (s-cis conformation required) + dienophile (electron-poor alkene) → cyclohexene. Stereochemistry: syn-addition, endo rule (endo product kinetically preferred with bridged systems). Diene: electron-rich (EDG); Dienophile: electron-poor (carbonyl, nitrile, NO2). Concerted — no intermediates

Enantiomers: non-superimposable mirror images; same physical properties except optical rotation (rotate plane-polarized light equally and oppositely). Diastereomers: same connectivity, different configuration at ≥ 1 stereocenter, NOT mirror images. Different physical properties. Meso compound: stereoisomer with internal mirror plane (achiral despite stereocenters)

Cahn-Ingold-Prelog: assign priorities 1–4 based on atomic number (highest → 1). Arrange lowest priority (4) away from viewer. Clockwise 1→2→3: R. Counterclockwise: S. When lowest priority is toward viewer, reverse the designation. Double-bond/ring atoms: phantom atoms at each end with same element

E (entgegen = opposite): higher priority groups on opposite sides of double bond. Z (zusammen = same): higher priority groups on same side. CIP rules used for priority. Cis/trans: only valid when both carbons have identical substituent. E/Z is always unambiguous
[α] = α / (c × l). α = observed rotation (degrees). c = concentration (g/mL). l = path length (dm). Enantiomeric excess (ee) = ([α]mixture / [α]pure) × 100%. Racemic mixture (50:50): [α] = 0

pKa order (more acidic = lower pKa): carboxylic acids (~4–5) > phenols (~10) > alcohols (~16) > terminal alkynes (~25) > alkynes (~25) > NH3 (~38) > alkanes (~50). Carbanion stability = conjugate base stability. More substituted carbocation but less stable carbanion

cards.front: Carbonyl chemistry: inductive vs resonance effects on electrophilicity
cards.back: Carbonyl C is electrophilic. Electron-withdrawing groups (halogens, other carbonyls) ↑ electrophilicity. Resonance donation (NR2 in amides) ↓ electrophilicity → amides are least reactive acyl derivatives. Electron density on O through resonance: ketone > ester > amide (decreasing nucleophilicity of O)

cards.front: Gabriel synthesis: what it makes and how
cards.back: Synthesis of primary amines (no secondary contamination). Phthalimide anion (nucleophile) alkylates R-X (SN2) → N-alkylphthalimide → hydrolysis (acid or hydrazine) → primary amine + phthalic acid/phthalhydrazide. Useful for clean synthesis of 1° amines

Positive result (yellow CHI3 precipitate) for: methyl ketones (RCOCH3), acetaldehyde (CH3CHO), and secondary alcohols that can be oxidized to methyl ketones. I2 + NaOH conditions halogenate α-carbons, then cleave → CHI3. Used to distinguish methyl ketones from other ketones

Aldehyde/ketone + amine → imine (Schiff base) with H2O loss → reduce with NaBH3CN or H2/Pd → amine. NaBH3CN: mild enough to reduce iminium ion but not free ketone. Used to install secondary/tertiary amines. Key in drug synthesis and biochemistry (PLP-based transamination)

Phosphonium ylide (Ph3P=CHR) + aldehyde/ketone → alkene + Ph3P=O. Forms C=C in place of C=O. Stability of ylide determines product: stabilized ylide (EWG on α-C) → E-alkene (thermodynamic). Unstabilized ylide → Z-alkene (kinetic). Used when geometry of double bond must be controlled

cards.front: Nucleophilicity vs basicity: how to distinguish
cards.back: Nucleophilicity: rate of attack on electrophile (kinetic property, size and polarizability matter). Basicity: thermodynamic affinity for H+. In polar protic solvents: I− > Br− > Cl− > F− (nucleophilicity) but F− > Cl− > Br− > I− (basicity). In polar aprotic: nucleophilicity follows basicity (F− > others)

Stability: 3° > 2° > 1° > methyl. Factors: hyperconjugation, induction, resonance (allylic/benzylic most stable). Rearrangements: hydride (1,2-H shift) or methyl shift to form more stable carbocation. Pinacol rearrangement: 1,2-diol → ketone via carbocation intermediate

4n+2 π electrons (n = 0, 1, 2…): 2, 6, 10, 14... electrons. Cyclopentadienyl anion: 6π (aromatic, n=1). Cycloheptatrienyl cation: 6π (aromatic). Cyclobutadiene: 4π (antiaromatic). Benzene: 6π. Pyrrole: 6π (lone pair of N counts). Pyridine: 6π (lone pair of N does NOT count, in σ orbital)

cards.front: Carboxylic acid derivatives: hydrolysis reactivity rank
cards.back: Most reactive to hydrolysis → least: Acid chloride > Acid anhydride > Ester > Amide. Kinetics determined by leaving group stability and resonance stabilization. Acyl chlorides: rapid with water. Amides: require prolonged acid/base heating (stability explains peptide bond stability in biology)

cards.front: Epoxide ring-opening: acid vs base conditions
cards.back: Acid conditions (H3O+): protonates epoxide → SN1-like at more substituted carbon (Markovnikov). Base/nucleophile (NaOH): SN2 backside attack at less hindered (less substituted) carbon (anti-Markovnikov). Anti addition in both cases. Product: trans diol (anti addition)

Alkane (C-H bonds, most reduced) → Alcohol → Aldehyde/Ketone → Carboxylic Acid/Ester → CO2 (most oxidized). Adding O or removing H = oxidation. Removing O or adding H = reduction. KMnO4 oxidizes alkenes → diols or cleavage to carboxylic acids/ketones (harsh)

cards.front: Protecting groups: common examples and purpose
cards.back: Protect functional groups during multistep synthesis to prevent unwanted reactions. Alcohol → TMS ether (remove with F−), THP ether. Alcohol → acetate (remove with mild base). Amine → Boc carbamate (remove with TFA/HCl), Cbz (remove by H2/Pd). Aldehyde → acetal (remove with aqueous acid)

0–2 ppm: alkyl C-H. 2–3 ppm: α to carbonyl. 3.5–4.5 ppm: O-CH, N-CH. 4.5–6.5 ppm: vinyl C-H (alkene). 6.5–8.5 ppm: aromatic C-H. 9–10 ppm: aldehyde C-H. 10–13 ppm: carboxylic acid OH. Spin-spin coupling: n+1 rule (n neighboring H → n+1 peaks)

3300–3500 cm⁻¹ (broad): O-H stretch (alcohol/acid). 3300 cm⁻¹ (sharp): N-H. 2700–2850 cm⁻¹: aldehyde C-H (two bands). 2200–2260 cm⁻¹: C≡N (nitrile) or C≡C. 1700–1750 cm⁻¹: C=O (ketone ~1715, ester ~1735, acid ~1710, amide ~1680). Absence of carbonyl + broad OH: alcohol

cards.front: Amino acids: classifications and pKa values
cards.back: 20 standard amino acids. pKa: α-COOH ~2, α-NH3+ ~9–10. Isoelectric point (pI) = (pKa1 + pKa2) / 2 for simple amino acids. Basic AA (Arg, Lys, His) have pI > 7; acidic AA (Asp, Glu) have pI < 7. Separation by electrophoresis at pH = pI: no net movement

Partial double bond character (C-N resonance) → planar, rigid, restricted rotation. Typically trans (Z-isomer not formed). Hydrolysis: requires acid/base/protease. Peptide bond torsion angles: φ (phi, Cα–N rotation) and ψ (psi, Cα–C rotation) define protein secondary structure (Ramachandran plot)

Quaternary ammonium salt + Ag2O/H2O → exhaustive methylation → Hofmann elimination. Produces less substituted alkene (anti-Markovnikov), unlike E2 with Zaitsev. E2 geometry still required (anti-periplanar). Used in structure elucidation of natural products with nitrogen

Selectively halogenates the α-carbon of carboxylic acids. Conditions: carboxylic acid + Br2 (or Cl2) + PBr3 (or PCl3) catalyst → α-halo acid. Mechanism: acid anhydride/acid halide intermediate → enolizes → electrophilic halogenation. Useful for making α-amino acids via substitution of halide with NH3

Electrolysis of carboxylate salt → radical at anode → decarboxylation → alkyl radical → dimerization → alkane. RCOO- → R• + CO2 → 2R• → R-R. Used for symmetric alkane synthesis. Decarboxylation is also used in biosynthesis (malonate, malonyl-CoA)

cards.front: Oxidation of alcohols: reagents and products
cards.back: Primary alcohol → aldehyde: PCC (pyridinium chlorochromate) or Swern (no overoxidation to acid). Primary → carboxylic acid: KMnO4, CrO3/H2SO4, Jones reagent. Secondary → ketone: same reagents. Tertiary alcohols: cannot be oxidized (no α-H on carbon bearing OH). Alcohol + O3/Me2S = ozonolysis (for alkenes, not alcohols)

cards.front: Reduction of functional groups: NaBH4 vs LiAlH4
cards.back: NaBH4: mild — reduces aldehydes/ketones → alcohols. Does NOT reduce esters, carboxylic acids, amides, or C=C. LiAlH4: strong — reduces all carbonyl groups (acids → primary alcohols, esters → 2 alcohols, amides → amines, nitriles → amines). Reacts violently with water — use in anhydrous ether/THF

cards.front: Michael addition: what it is and example
cards.back: 1,4-conjugate addition of nucleophile (Michael donor) to α,β-unsaturated carbonyl (Michael acceptor). Michael donor: enolate, malonate, cyanide, amine. Product: 1,4-addition product (β-functionalized carbonyl). Often followed by Claisen condensation = Robinson annulation → 6-membered ring

Michael addition + aldol condensation. Michael addition of enolate to enone → diketone → intramolecular aldol → dehydration → 2-cyclohexenone ring system. Used to build 6-membered carbocyclic rings in natural product synthesis (steroids, terpenoids)

Oxime (ketone + NH2OH) treated with strong acid (H2SO4, PCl5) → rearrangement → amide. The anti group (opposite to OH in oxime) migrates. Product: cyclic amide (lactam) when cyclic ketone is used. Industrial significance: Beckmann rearrangement of cyclohexanone oxime → caprolactam → nylon-6

Ketone + peracid (mCPBA, H2O2/acid) → ester or lactone. The more substituted group migrates preferentially (3° > 2° > Ph > CH3). For cyclic ketones → lactones. Mechanism: peracid attacks carbonyl → Criegee intermediate → migration. Useful in synthesis and steroid chemistry

Cope rearrangement: [3,3]-sigmatropic rearrangement of 1,5-diene → isomeric 1,5-diene. Oxy-Cope: 1,5-dien-3-ol → enol → ketone (driven thermodynamically by enol-ketone tautomerism — makes reaction irreversible). Both are concerted, chair-like transition states, stereospecific

cards.front: Pinacol rearrangement: substrate and driving force
cards.back: 1,2-diol (pinacol) treated with acid → dehydration of one OH → tertiary carbocation → 1,2-shift of alkyl/aryl group or H → ketone (pinacolone). Driving force: conversion of 2° carbocation to stable tertiary oxocarbenium ion (resonance-stabilized). Classic example: pinacol → pinacolone

Aldehyde/ketone + 2 equivalents alcohol + acid catalyst (H+) ⇌ acetal + H2O. Equilibrium driven by removing water. Purpose: protect carbonyl group (stable to base, nucleophiles, reducing agents; not stable to dilute acid). Cyclic acetals (ethylene glycol) more stable and preferred. Remove: dilute aqueous acid

Convert enantiomers to diastereomers using a chiral resolving agent → separate diastereomers (different mp, solubility) → remove resolving agent → recover individual enantiomers. Example: racemic acid + chiral amine → diastereomeric salts → crystallize → separate → free acids. Modern: chiral HPLC columns

cards.front: Neighboring group participation: effect on mechanism
cards.back: An internal nucleophile (adjacent functional group) assists in displacement — forms bridged cyclic intermediate. Effect: retention of configuration (double inversion). Example: α-haloester or β-amino alcohol can cyclize to form episulfonium, aziridinium, or epoxide intermediate. Changes expected product stereochemistry

Requires electron-withdrawing groups ortho/para to leaving group (activate ring toward nucleophilic attack). Meisenheimer complex (anionic intermediate). Example: 2,4-dinitrochlorobenzene + NaOH → 2,4-dinitrophenol. Rate increased by more EWG groups. Used in synthesis of nitrophenols, aryl ethers

cards.front: Spectroscopic identification: degree of unsaturation formula
cards.back: DoU (Index of Hydrogen Deficiency) = (2C + 2 + N − H − X) / 2. Each double bond = 1 DoU. Ring = 1 DoU. Triple bond = 2 DoU. Benzene = 4 DoU (3 double bonds + 1 ring). N adds 1 to numerator; O has no effect; halogens counted as H. Use to determine possible structural features from molecular formula
